feed.title: SAP Community - Artificial Intelligence
feed.subtitle: Artificial Intelligence blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Why ERP still defines the limits of AI in the enterprise
entry.content:
Introduction

There’s been a lot of noise around AI and how much it’s changing enterprise systems. It is undeniable that changes are here to stay. We’ve seen teams getting better forecasts, automating things that used to take forever, and catching issues earlier than before. Even we as an enterprise have evolved into something that I have no idea how it would function if AI suddenly disappeared one day.

But after a while, the conversation shifts. You can do countless of things with AI, and I think that is what everyone else is focusing on. And what else? What comes after checking whether AI works or not? What do you actually do once it's there? Getting recommendations is not the hard part anymore. You can have a model suggesting changes, flagging risks, or pointing at opportunities, and most of the time it makes sense. The problem shows up later, when you try to use that in real processes. That’s usually where things start to slow down, and where ERP suddenly matters again.

The Gap Between AI Insights and Execution

We’ve seen this happen more than once. Companies expect faster decisions because they have better insights, but once those decisions need to move through finance, operations, or logistics, everything gets more complicated. Systems don’t always adapt quickly, processes depend on each other, and what looked like a simple adjustment ends up taking way more effort than expected.

In many scenarios, the real challenge emerges when you have to close the execution gap, and not when you have to add more intelligence, as many people may think. It’s something that comes up all the time in conversations with our clients: the need is there, the intent is clear, but the underlying ERP landscape just isn’t ready to move at the same speed. Closing that gap between expectation and reality is where most of the real work is happening today.

The struggle that most organizations have has todo with what comes after they have produce insights. How do you execute a decision through a system that is so structured and interconnected? Is thanks to SAP S/4HANA —and similar ERP platforms— that decision has the opportunity to become reality.

On paper, the issue is simple. AI generates an insight, the business evaluates it, and then we, as organization, execute it. The thing is that, in reality, this chain encounters a vast amount of problems that lead to decisions getting delayed or partially implemented.

In one of the projects we worked on, this became very clear. The company, a global manufacturer in the energy space, was still relying on manual processes to count and track critical components. It wasn’t just slow, it also introduced errors and even safety concerns for the people involved.

By introducing AI-based image recognition, they were able to automate this process and significantly improve both productivity and safety. However, what became clear during the implementation is that the real value didn’t come from the AI model itself, but from how that information was integrated and executed across the operation. The data captured in the field needed to be synchronized with ERP systems, trigger updates in inventory, and align with warehouse and logistics processes in real time. Without that connection, the insight would have remained isolated. It was the ability to translate that intelligence into structured actions within the system that ultimately made the difference.

In another case, a client was using predictive models to anticipate demand changes and adjust procurement accordingly. The insights were accurate, but execution lagged behind. Translating those recommendations into actual purchase orders required multiple validations and manual interventions within the ERP system. By the time the adjustments were fully implemented, the conditions had already shifted. Once again, the limitation was not the quality of the insight, but the ability to execute it in time.

This is why the conversation around ERP is becoming relevant again, especially in the context of AI. It’s no longer just about having a system to run transactions, but about having a system that can absorb change and execute decisions without friction. Concepts like clean core, standardized processes, and flexible extensions are not just technical preferences anymore. They directly impact how fast and how effectively a company can act on what AI is telling them.

Conclusion

What we are seeing, then, is a shift in how these technologies relate to each other. AI is expanding what companies can see and understand about their business, giving them more options and better insights. ERP, on the other hand, continues to define what they can actually do with that information. The gap between those two layers is where many organizations are currently stuck.

The truth is that the companies that will get the most value out of AI are the ones that connect with the models to execution, and not necessarily the ones with the most advanced models. That means treating ERP not as a static backend system, but as a dynamic platform that enables change across the organization. AI might be redefining how companies think, but ERP still defines how they act. In the enterprise, action is where value is created.

entry.title: Exploring AI-Powered SAP Integrations using SAP Integration Suite and LLM APIs
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence is rapidly becoming part of modern enterprise architectures. As SAP Integration professionals, one interesting area to explore is how integration platforms can orchestrate AI-enabled workflows.

As part of my recent learning journey, I explored how SAP Integration Suite can integrate with Large Language Models (LLMs) and built a simple AI-powered Error Resolver demo using SAP Cloud Integration and Groq APIs.

Understanding the Basics

Before implementing the solution, it is important to understand a few key concepts.

What are LLMs?

Large Language Models (LLMs) are AI models trained on large amounts of textual data. Examples include GPT models, Llama models, Gemini, and Claude.

These models can:

  • Understand prompts
  • Generate responses
  • Summarize information
  • And assist with troubleshooting and analysis

What is RAG?

RAG stands for Retrieval Augmented Generation.

In a RAG architecture:

  1. Relevant context is retrieved
  2. The context is added to the prompt
  3. The LLM generates a more contextual and intelligent response

This improves the quality and relevance of AI-generated answers.

What are Embeddings and Vector Databases?

Embeddings are numerical vector representations of text.

These embeddings are usually stored in Vector Databases such as:

  • SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine
  • Pinecone
  • Weaviate
  • And others

The Vector Database helps retrieve semantically similar information during AI processing.

For this demo, I intentionally simplified the retrieval process using Groovy-based logic instead of implementing a real Vector Database. This allowed me to focus first on orchestration and AI integration concepts.

Integration as an Orchestration Layer

One of the most interesting learnings from this exercise was understanding the role of SAP Integration Suite as an orchestration layer.

Even though:

  • The LLM runs externally
  • Embeddings may be generated externally
  • Retrieval may happen through a Vector Database

The orchestration still remains controlled by SAP Cloud Integration.

The integration flow can manage:

  • Prompt preparation
  • API communication
  • Contextual augmentation
  • Response parsing
  • Final response handling

This makes SAP Integration Suite highly relevant in future AI-enabled enterprise architectures.

Demo Use Case – AI-Powered Error Resolver

The demo use case was intentionally kept simple.

Input Example:
“Mapping failed: InvoiceID missing”

The iFlow:

  1. Receives the error
  2. Builds contextual information
  3. Sends the prompt to a Groq-hosted LLM model
  4. Receives an AI-generated response
  5. Returns the probable root cause and resolution

Technologies Used

The demo was implemented using:

  • SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP Cloud Integration (SAP CI)
  • Groovy Scripts
  • Groq APIs
  • Llama 3.3 70B model
  • POSTMAN for API validation

High-Level Architecture

SAP CI → Prompt Orchestration → Groq LLM API → AI Response Parsing → Final Resolution

At this stage, retrieval was simulated through Groovy logic instead of a real Vector Database.

Key Learnings from this Exercise

Some important learnings from this exploration:

  • AI integrations are fundamentally orchestration-driven.
  • Prompt quality significantly impacts AI responses.
  • Integration platforms can become powerful AI orchestration layers.
  • Understanding APIs and response structures is critical.
  • Starting simple helps in understanding AI architecture practically.

Demo Video

iFlow Design

 

Final Thoughts

This exploration was a great starting point in understanding how SAP Integration landscapes may evolve alongside Generative AI technologies.

There is still much more to learn about:

  • Embeddings
  • Vector databases
  • AI governance
  • SAP AI Core
  • Production-grade RAG architectures.

However, building small practical demos is an excellent way to understand how these technologies fit together in real enterprise scenarios.

The goal is not to become an overnight AI expert, but to continuously explore how modern AI capabilities can complement enterprise integration architectures.

entry.title: New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live
entry.content:
TLDR: The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey is now live on learning.sap and alongside it the New Role Play based SAP Certified Data Architect certification is now available in this learning Journey: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect

Data Architecture Learning Begins here!

Early in my career, I sat in a room full of architects debating a major platform decision. The conversation covered integration patterns, application landscapes, and total cost of ownership. No one discussed the data. Not the quality of it, not how it would flow, not how it would be governed. We treated data as the responsibility of someone else (master data team and BW team) in a different meeting. Years later, we are seeing AI initiatives slow down because of untrustworthy data, I understand exactly what was missing from that room.

Data architecture has spent years as a supporting discipline important, but rarely the headline. That has changed. The shift to agentic AI has moved data architecture from a back-office concern to a front-line competitive capability.

Agentic AI systems do not simply retrieve information. They reason, plan, and act. They require data that is real-time, contextually grounded, semantically rich, and governed with precision. An agent operating on stale, fragmented, or poorly modelled data does not just produce mediocre outputs it can produce incorrect actions. At enterprise scale, that is a governance and business risk problem, not just a technical one.

The architects who will lead in this environment are those who can hold both conversations at once: the strategic (how data drives business value) and the technical (how to design the platforms, integration patterns, and governance structures that make that value real).  

That is exactly what we have tried to address with the New Data Architecture learning Course (Advanced) and a New Data Architect Certification from SAP

Introducing the Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Course

The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey (course BDCDA2) is now live on learning.sap.com. Some of you might have done ‘Becoming a Data Architect – Foundational Course’ which was launched last year and this new course takes a deeper look at the topics related to Data Architecture.

Across four-unit journey designed for architects who are ready to move beyond foundational concepts and into the architecture decisions, framework synthesis, and platform-specific depth that define effective data leadership in the agentic AI era.

  1.  Unit 1: Architecting Data Strategy for the Agentic AI Era

The journey opens with strategy, because that is where most data initiatives fail first. Unit 1 builds the five foundational pillars of data strategy: Governance, Data literacy, Stewardship, Architecture, and Technology enablement and connects each directly to AI readiness. It examines what agentic AI demands from data pipelines that traditional analytics architectures were not built to provide: real-time streaming, semantic enrichment, and contextual grounding. It then moves to leadership and culture, the organizational factors that determine whether a data strategy survives contact with reality and closes with a framework for measuring data strategy success and analyzing real-world value patterns.

  1. Unit 2: Integrating Frameworks for Data Architecture

Architecture without a framework produces inconsistency. Unit 2 examines the three frameworks that define the professional standard in this field: TOGAF, DAMA-DMBOK, and the SAP Enterprise Architecture Framework and, guides how to synthesize them rather than apply them in isolation. It then applies that synthesis to two of the most significant architectural paradigms in the market today: Data Mesh and Data Fabric.

  1.  Unit 3: Harmonizing SAP Business Data Cloud Within the Open Data Ecosystem

Unit 3 is where strategy and frameworks meet the SAP platform. It begins with BDC Connect and the Delta Sharing protocol the mechanism that enables zero-copy data sharing across ecosystem boundaries and then works through each major integration scenario in the open data ecosystem: Databricks, Snowflake (including all three connectivity options), Microsoft Fabric, and Google Big Query. The unit closes with a dedicated lesson on data federation performance optimization the practical trade-offs between federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing that every architect advising on SAP Business Data Cloud deployments needs to understand cold.

  1. Unit 4: Designing Advanced Data Models and Architecture for SAP Business Data Cloud

The final unit addresses SAP Business Data Cloud as a Business Data Fabric how it implements federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing to connect heterogeneous data sources while preserving business context and semantic coherence. It covers Data Mesh in SAP BDC, including how Datasphere Spaces implement the four Data Mesh principles in practice. It then moves to one of the most consequential topics in enterprise AI architecture: semantic models and knowledge graphs as the foundation for trustworthy AI and RAG-based systems.

 Introducing the SAP Certified Data Architect Certification

Alongside the learning journey, we have launched the SAP Certified Data Architect certification.

The SAP Certified Data Architect is an AI role-play-based certification that assesses how well candidates understand modern data architecture in the context of strategic business success. It covers the five major architectural paradigms: Data Warehouse, Data Lake, Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Data Lakehouse, alongside the core elements of data maturity: governance frameworks, data security, and business-aligned data strategy. It uses interactive question-and-answer format, assessing the understanding of SAP Data Architecture in the agentic AI era specifically the ability to synthesize and articulate how SAP Business Data Cloud enables governed, semantically coherent data access across hybrid cloud environments.

Note: It is recommended that the candidate completes the 1) Becoming an SAP Data Architect and 2) Mastering SAP Data Architecture courses before attempting the certificate to ensure successful completion.

 Who This Is For

The learning journey and certification are designed for practitioners who are already working in the data and architecture space and want to develop the rigor, depth, and formal recognition to operate at senior level. That includes data architects designing enterprise data platforms, enterprise architects expanding their data architecture competency, analytics and BI leaders responsible for governed data access, and technical decision-makers advising customers on SAP Business Data Cloud strategy.

 If you are starting your data architecture journey, the foundational 'Becoming a Data Architect' learning journey on learning.sap.com is the right starting point. The advanced journey picks up where that one ends.

Getting Started

  1. The Becoming a Certified SAP Data Architect Learning Journey live now for both internal and external learners: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect
  2. SAP Certified Data Architect certification registration: https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-data-architect-sap-business-data-cloud

A Final Thought

In the agentic AI era, the constraint on enterprise intelligence is rarely the algorithm. It is the architecture underneath it the decisions made about how data is structured, governed, connected, and made meaningful. Those decisions require a specific set of skills. This learning journey and certification exist to build them.

The practitioners who invest in that depth now will be the ones designing the data foundations that AI-enabled enterprises run on. That work is not infrastructure. It is strategy.

Happy Learning!

P.S: A Big Thank you to @JaSoN_Luo , Sabrina Brück and @Basabdatta_Sengupta  for making this course and certification Happen!

SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live
entry.content:

entry.title: Must-See Utilities Highlights at SAP Sapphire Madrid
entry.content:
Dear Utilities Community,

SAP Sapphire kicked off last week in Orlando where SAP CEO Christian Klein announced the Business AI Platform as the foundation for the Autonomous Enterprise,  enabling AI agents to run end‑to‑end business processes with deep business context, governance, and security. SAP’s vision goes beyond traditional ERP by embedding AI directly into mission‑critical workflows, helping organizations turn AI investments into tangible, accurate, and scalable business outcomes.

Explore the SAP Sapphire 2026 news and innovations and watch keynotes on demand. 

 

This week, SAP Sapphire continues in Madrid. While the core content remains similar, discussions often take on a more European perspective, with a stronger focus on regulation, regional market dynamics, and local customer success stories.
 
There are many exciting sessions—I'd especially like to highlight three Utilities-specific highlights, all taking place on Wednesday, May 20.

Ignite the future: Accelerate your energy transition | IND1261 at 10:30 CEST in Hall 9, Industries Center, Theater 

The global race toward energy transition presents not only complex challenges but also unprecedented opportunities for industry leaders and consumers. SAP’s groundbreaking innovations, real-world AI applications, and fresh partnerships are reshaping the landscape. Thrive in a rapidly evolving market and be at the forefront of the energy revolution. Join Filip Kowalski's (MEE Industry Lead for Utilities and Oil, Gas & Energy) presentation. 

National Grid transforms capital investment accounting with SAP BTP and AI | BTP1483 at 1:00 PM CEST in Hall 10, Theater 10

Discover how agentic AI is enabling the next generation of capital investment accounting with SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and AI. Hear how AI-powered agents at British energy company National Grid plc help autonomously orchestrate processes, improve financial transparency, and support faster, more-informed investment decisions. The session will be presented by Aley Matthewson, SAP Functional Manager at National Grid and Blake Goddard, Senior Business AI Platform Executive at SAP. 

Industry AI: Reimagining core processes across every industry | IND1407 at 1:30 in Hall 10, Theater 11

Across industries, businesses have the same goal: find ways to use AI that actually deliver on its promise. See what next-generation industry innovation looks like and how SAP helps customers execute on it. Discover two new use cases codeveloped with pharmaceutical packaging leader Uhlmann Group and premier professional services firm PwC.
Speakers: Robert Brachman, Head fo Business Engineering, Uhlmann Pac-Systeme; Jill Notz, MX Controller, PwC; Thomas Ruhl, CPO Industries, SAP; Andre Bechthold, President SAP Industries & Experiences, SAP; 

RWE: AI-driven offshore wind troubleshooting | IND1609 at 3:30 PM CEST in Hall 10, Theater 14 

RWE uses AI in SAP solutions to speed offshore wind repairs and boost asset uptime. Technicians deploy AI-assisted SAP solutions to analyze past notes and OEM manuals, pinpoint root causes, and draft tailored repair work orders. Field technicians get support to improve repair documentation and feed insights into the system for continuous learning.

Join the session from Frank Scholtka, Director Operations UK at RWE Offshore Wind GmbH and Bruno Pincolini, Global Lead for SAP Business AI for Utilities at SAP.

 
Join us also at the Utilities Industry Expert Station in Hall 9 (Industries Center – IND1520) to connect 1:1 with SAP specialists and explore your key challenges and opportunities. Discover how AI is reshaping industry processes at the Experience Hub (EXP1536), and gain valuable insights into SAP’s portfolio during the Utilities & Oil & Gas Industry Tour. Reach out to your SAP Account Manager to reserve your spot and make the most of these tailored experiences.

Visit the SAP Sapphire Madrid website to explore topics and build your personal schedule. 

I am looking forward to hearing your feedback.

Best regards,

Nicole

entry.title: Connect LLM to SAP with MCP - Part 1
entry.content:
In this blog I go over the scenario of creating an agent that based on user queries interprets them, translates mails and content to business partner and material, and then creates a sales order in the system. This is part 1, in parts 2 and 3 I will extend this to a Cowork scenario where the human player is removed and I add OAuth2 authentication for full audit capability of the process.

I'm not using fancy integrations with SAP BTP in this scenario because I have several smaller customers that simply don't have that, they still use user and password authentication, no IaS, no integration suite. They just want to use AI with what they have.  I'll upgrade this MCP in follow ups, with the most important thing, user based authentication at scale, which secures audit logs, so stay tuned. 

Not only does this blog show you how AI can managed the process it shows how AI can build the entire thing, being the most important skill today: 

  • Knowing what can be done with AI
  • Knowing what business process works, where the information is, and how to use it. 

I have a video tutorial which makes this easier to watch but in essence the MCP is doing 3 things: 

  • Translating an email to business partner ID using a OData call; 
  • Translating descriptions of material to material ID using local vector database. Here I show you how you can do similarity searches so that the LLM rejects low value results. 
  • Creates the sales order with the transactional data created.

Then I show this MCP integrated with Claude, but it could be any agent, even SAP Joule as you can connect MCP to Joule Skills in the studio. By the way question to the audience, does Joule have an out of the box skill to create sales orders? Comment below to let me know as I get conflicting info. 

Additional important remarks:

  • Claude Code does make some errors still especially with fields in the OData service so I did have to make some fixes, but I never touched the code, I just fed the information it lacked back to it, and it fixed everything.
  • Why didn't I use something like the community OData-Explorer MCP that makes OData services available to LLMs? Honestly, lack of control, LLM still make mistakes when calling APIs and I want to make sure the translation to the API call is safer and more reliable. I also wanted to implement local vector search for efficiency sake and similarity scores so It would be a no go. 

Finally, regarding the infamous API policy I won't go there in this blog. If you believe something like this is against the new policy I think every customer should a talk with their account managers to discuss the situation. I believe some AI use cases do present a risk to ERP stability but things like this are just replicating what a normal external application could also do. 

Comment below if there is a feature you would like me to add to the MCP. 

Thanks for reading

entry.title: Text Processing in NLP
entry.content:
Introduction:

NLP is a field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that allows computers to understand, interpret, and utilize human language in context. The reason human language is so complicated is primarily due to grammar, emotions, context words and terminologies of a particular field as well as different writing styles. The rise of Natural language processing:NLP enables machines to dissect this language and transmute unstructured text into structured data, which is easily consumable by computers.

Body:

Natural Language Processing employs a number of textual preprocessing methodologies including tokenization, lemmatization, vectorization and similarity modeling. These techniques are used to split the text into smaller pieces (such as words or sentences), extract root words, convert that text into a numerical vector and compare meaning between two sentences/sentences/documents. Further, after the data is processed it can feed machine learning and deep learning models for intelligent decision making and predictions.

Tokenization :

Tokenization breaks down a given sentence or text into smaller parts called tokens. Computers cannot read human sentence as a whole. Hence, first NLP divides the text into smaller chunks for processing.

Example:

Input: “NLP is very interesting”

Output : ["NLP", "is", "very", "interesting"]

Lemmatization

Lemmatization is the process of converting a word to its base or root form known as a lemma. This allows NLP systems to comprehend that different variants of a term signify the same thing.

Input :["running", "studies", "better"]

Output : ["run", "study", "good"]

Vectorization

Vectorization is the process of converting text or words into numerical form (vectors) so that machine learning models can understand and process them.TF-IDF Vectorization is an NLP technique used to convert text into numerical vectors based on the importance of words in a document. TF-IDF helps identify which words are important and meaningful in a sentence or document

Input

["I love NLP", "NLP is interesting"]

Output : 

[

 [1, 1, 1, 0],

 [0, 1, 0, 1]

]

Find similarity:

In NLP, finding similarity is the task of two texts to see how similar they are.It teaches computers to understand when two sentences are saying the same thing.Word Embeddings, Cosine Similarity and TF-IDF are commonly used techniques. Typically, the similarity score is between 0 and 1.

Input

Sentence 1: "I love NLP"

Sentence 2: "I like NLP"

 

Output

0.82

 

Summary: 

NLP is widely used these days in chatbots, virtual assistants, search engines, translation systems, sentiment analysis, recommendation systems, spam filtering, and AI-powered enterprise applications. Popular applications include Siri, Alexa, Google Translate, ChatGPT and customer service bots. NLP is a key factor in enhancing human-computer interaction and automating language-based tasks in different sectors.

entry.title: AI Needs Clean Data. Business Networks Stop It Getting Dirty in the First Place
entry.content:
There is a concept in professional kitchens called mise en place, everything in its place before service begins. The best chefs do not start cooking and hope the ingredients sort themselves out mid-order. The prep happens first, deliberately and completely, so that when the pressure is on the kitchen can move at pace without chaos.

For some reason my brain decided to align that principle to the concept of data quality and its use in agentic AI. I finally figured out what caused the spark, Zero100's AI-Ready Data Imperative report which defines the material difference between data that informs a decision, data that triggers a recommendation, and data that an agent acts on autonomously (there is also a nice podcast on the topic). A dashboard built on imperfect data gets questioned by a human who applies judgement and context. An agent built on imperfect data acts, reroutes the shipment, adjusts the replenishment signal, commits to the supplier, before anyone has noticed the input was wrong. The same ambiguity that a person absorbs in a meeting becomes operational risk the moment a system is doing the acting.

The same research found that only 15% of organisations consider their data foundations execution-ready. Let that land for a moment, because that means 85% of the AI programmes being announced, funded, and celebrated right now are being built on ground that cannot support the weight being placed on them.

The honest reality is that the foundations have not been prepared for this level of trust, and most organisations are not yet having that conversation clearly enough to know it.

The instinctive response is to treat this as a reason to delay. Clean everything first, then deploy agents. That instinct is understandable, but most organisations do not have the luxury of acting on it. Board pressure is intense, Wall Street is rewarding AI narratives and punishing silence, and vendors are racing to rebadge everything as agentic. The result is a top-down mandate to move fast colliding with a bottom-up reality that the foundations are not ready. Waiting for the data estate to be fully clean is not a strategy, it is an indefinite deferral dressed up as diligence. I have yet to meet the enterprise that finished a data quality programme and declared victory.

So the real question is not how do we clean everything but what do we actually need to clean to make a specific decision trustworthy.

Zero100's research consistently finds that the list of data inputs that actually determine the outcome of any given decision is shorter than most people expect. Work backwards from the decision, inventory rebalancing, late shipment triage, supplier payment timing, and ask which fields, if wrong or late, make the agent's action wrong or late. Typically ten to twenty fields. They call these Golden Inputs, not because they are glamorous, but because they carry disproportionate weight and deserve disproportionate attention. Govern those fields with the same rigour applied to operational KPIs, and you have a foundation an agent can genuinely act on. Leave them in the same state as everything else, and you have an agent making fast, confident, wrong decisions.

Here is where the argument takes a turn that most conversations about data readiness miss entirely. In supply chain, the triggering event is almost always external. A supplier confirmation, a carrier delay, an invoice exception, a forecast revision. The decisions agents will be asked to make are not primarily driven by data that originates inside your four walls. They are driven by signals arriving from outside them. Which means the most consequential Golden Inputs in any supply chain decision are very often external data fields, not internal ones.

This matters because it reframes the entire data cleansing challenge. Internal data cleansing is valuable but it is a continuous battle, because dirty data keeps entering the estate faster than it can be cleaned. Every new transaction, every supplier update, every manual entry is a potential source of contamination. It is the culinary equivalent of trying to keep a kitchen clean while unchecked deliveries arrive throughout service.

Business networks change that dynamic fundamentally. Data arriving over a governed network is structured, validated, and clean at the point of entry. Those fields do not join the cleansing backlog because they never needed cleansing in the first place. Think of it as the difference between a kitchen that inspects every ingredient as it arrives from the supplier, and one that waits until service to discover the fish was not fresh. Quality is established before it enters the kitchen, not discovered as a problem halfway through cooking.

This is where SAP Business Network plays a defining role. With millions of trading partners already connected and transacting through structured, governed document flows, the network is delivering AI-ready Golden Inputs at scale today, not as a future state. Purchase orders, advance ship notices, invoices, confirmations, each carrying their critical fields, all arriving clean by design. For organisations building toward agentic AI in supply chain, that is not a nice-to-have, it is the shortest path between ambition and trustworthy autonomous action.

The path to agentic AI is not a transformation programme. It is a sequence of small, governed steps, each one earning the right to the next. Start with a single decision, identify its Golden Inputs, and rather than beginning an internal cleansing project of uncertain duration, ask which of those inputs arrives via a business network and is therefore already clean, already structured, already trustworthy. In supply chain that answer is often most of them.

The problem in most kitchens is not the quality of the chefs. It is that the ingredients keep arriving without quality checks, and the team spends half its time sorting through the delivery before it can start cooking. The best kitchens do not just prep well, they have rigorous supplier standards, so the ingredients arrive ready to use.

That is what business networks do for agentic AI. Not a better mop. A cleaner kitchen.

entry.title: New Webinar Series: Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise - for Architects, by Architects
entry.content:
Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise: A Webinar Series for Architects, by Architects

TLDR; An 11-part webinar series unpacking SAP's Autonomous Enterprise for Architects covering Joule Work, the Autonomous Suite, the Business AI Platform, Knowledge Graph, AI agents, and the SAP Family of Models and what it all means for architects!

Starting: 1st June to 12 August 2026, weekly, 17:00–18:00 Europe / 20:30–21:30 India / 11:00–12:00 New York / 08:00–09:00 Palo Alto Time.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise: A Webinar Series for Architects, by Architects

Architecture is entering a new era. AI is moving beyond isolated copilots and individual experiments toward enterprise-wide orchestration, automation, and decision intelligence. That shift is not incremental it changes how enterprises are designed, governed, and operated in an AI-native world, and it places architects squarely at the centre of the conversation.

Following the latest announcements from Sapphire, we are pleased to launch Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise is an 11-part webinar series built for this moment.

Designed by architects, for architects, the series unpacks SAP's vision for the Autonomous Enterprise and translates it into the architectural choices, governance considerations, and adoption patterns that practitioners will actually have to make.

The announcements from SAP Sapphire have moved the conversation about enterprise AI from possibility to architecture. Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite, the SAP Business AI Platform, Knowledge Graph, AI agents, and the SAP Family of Models are no longer roadmap items. They are concrete capabilities that architects will be expected to design with, govern, secure, operationalise, and scale.

With this series, we aim to provide architect's a clear view of how all parts of an Autonomous Enterprise fit together, where the design decisions actually sit, and what changes in the role of the architect when AI agents become first-class citizens in the enterprise landscape.

This series is structured to close that gap, taking participants from the strategic Why through the architectural and technical How, without skipping the messy parts in between.

What makes this series different

This is not a product walkthrough. Each session is led by practitioners, product leaders, customers or guest speakers who are building, governing, or advising customers on the same architectural choices you are working through.

The format prioritises depth over breadth: one focused topic per session, with room for real Q&A.

The series brings together SAP architects, product teams, customers, and external industry experts including a customer perspectives on SAP Family of Models, View on data foundations, and an external governance perspective from Steve Tuppen of Mozaic, alongside a keynote from Dr. Sebastian Wernicke, author of Data Inspired and TED speaker. We also have SAP Leaders such as Andre Christ and Mark Smith joining to share their strategic PoV on the topic!

The series runs from 1 June to 12 August 2026, with weekly one-hour sessions.

All sessions are held at 17:00–18:00 Europe / 20:30–21:30 India / 11:00–12:00 New York / 08:00–09:00 Palo Alto time zones.

 The Session Details Below:

# Session Title Speakers Date

1Unpacking SAP's Autonomous Enterprise for ArchitectsPaul Kurchina, Mark Smith, Gaurish Dessai, Jason Luo1 June 2026
2What SAP's Evolved Portfolio Means for ArchitectsPaul Kurchina, Ralph Richter, Surprise Speaker 🙂10 June 2026
3Architecting From Intent to Action with Joule Work & Knowledge GraphJacob Hornberger, Pavithra GK16 June 2026*
4Playbook for Building Your Own SAP AgentsJulia Kloppenburg, Gaurish Dessai26 June 2026
5From Patterns to Consumption: SAP's Innovation Factory for Architecting and Activating AI in SAP LandscapesHelmut Grimm, Raghu Banda, Jose Tornero, Vishal Mehta1 July 2026
6An Architect's Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management at Enterprise ScaleAndré Christ, Laurenz Vogt8 July 2026
7An Architect's Guide to the SAP Family of ModelsMarkus Kohler, Simon Knehr , Ashok Veilumuthu14 July 2026
8Data Foundations for an Autonomous EnterpriseNiclas Schlautkoetter, Dr. Sebastian Wernicke, Surprise Customer Speaker 22 July 2026
9From Autonomous Domains to Industry AI: One Platform, Two LensesRaghu Banda, Naveen Dumpa, Cristina Dan29 July 2026
10AI Governance and Security for an Autonomous EnterpriseSteve Tuppen (Mozaic), Dr. Michael . Blaschke, Stephan Wronkowski-Elster5 August 2026
11Role of an Enterprise Architect in Driving the Autonomous EnterprisePaul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch, Johannes Euler12 August 2026

* To be Finalised

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

How the series is structured?

The 11 sessions are sequenced deliberately.

The first three sessions establish the foundation. Session 1 unpacks the Autonomous Enterprise concept fresh from the Sapphire announcements and introduces the key components Joule Work, the Autonomous Suite, the Business AI Platform, and the SAP Family of Models. Session 2 examines how the full SAP portfolio comes together as a continuous, coherent capability rather than a one-time initiative, including the North Star and Golden Path AI framing. Session 3 then goes deep on Joule Work and the Knowledge Graph as the dynamic engagement layer that interprets intent, surfaces insights, and orchestrates AI agents across the enterprise.

Session 4 shifts from concept to construction. It is a hands-on playbook for building an SAP agent from scratch a show-and-tell session intended as a working reference for architects who need to demonstrate the stack to their own teams.

Sessions 5 through 9 go deeper into the architectural layers that matter most. Session 5 introduces SAP's Innovation Factory and the three connected threads of architecture patterns and persona-based discovery, the Innovation Factory as a repeatable approach, and the activation-to-consumption playbook. Session 6 tackles AI agent lifecycle management at enterprise scale how to discover, govern, secure, and measure the business impact of hundreds or thousands of autonomous agents without introducing agent sprawl, architectural drift, or unmanaged risk. Session 7 goes deep on the SAP Family of Models, including a customer example.

Session 8 brings together Dr. Sebastian Wernicke, Niclas Schlautkoetter on the role of the Data Architect and what is new with BDC, and a customer perspective anchored by the principle that the difference between isolated pilots and sustainable AI at scale lies in the foundational data architecture.

Session 9 closes this arc with autonomous domains organised around Finance, Supply Chain, Procurement, HCM, and Customer Engagement, alongside Reference Industry AI Kits that accelerate transformations with pre-built SAP best practice content.

The final two sessions close the loop. Session 10 brings in external perspective from Steve Tuppen of Mozaic on AI governance and security, alongside SAP experts on privacy, compliance, trust, clean-core principles, and interoperability across SAP and non-SAP environments defining guardrails for AI solutions that are effective, scalable, and governable.

Session 11 returns to the role itself: what the Autonomous Enterprise means for Enterprise Architects, how EAs architect decision-making across the enterprise using AI, and a wrap-up of highlights and next steps across the series together with Paul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch and Johannes Euler.

Why this matters for every architect discipline

The Autonomous Enterprise is not a single product decision. It is an architectural one, and it cuts across every discipline in the architect community. Enterprise Architects need a defensible view of how AI changes decision-making, value streams, and the operating model and how to govern that change without slowing it down.

Solution and Platform Architects need to know where Joule Work, agents, and the SAP Family of Models actually sit in a design, what they replace, and what they expose. Integration Architects are facing a landscape where agents and MCP servers are becoming first-class consumers and producers across the integration fabric, with real implications for orchestration, identity, and observability. Data and AI Architects sit at the centre of it all without a deliberate data foundation, AI initiatives stall in pilot mode regardless of how strong the application layer becomes. This series brings the four perspectives into the same conversation, in the same sequence, so the architectural picture lines up across roles rather than being assembled in fragments later.

Who should attend?

This series is designed for professionals shaping the future of enterprise transformation in an AI-native world:

  • Enterprise Architects driving business and technology transformation
  • Solution and Platform Architects modernising SAP landscapes and integration strategies
  • Data and AI Architects designing the foundations for intelligent, autonomous operations
  • Technology and Transformation Leaders responsible for AI adoption and innovation strategy
  • SAP Customers and Partners exploring how to operationalise AI across enterprise landscapes
  • Architecture and Governance Teams focused on scalability, security, compliance, and lifecycle management
  • Innovation and Business Technology Teams moving from AI pilots to enterprise-wide adoption

As the role of the architect becomes increasingly multidimensional spanning business strategy, data, AI, governance, platforms, security, and human-centric transformation this series is built to help architects navigate and lead across these interconnected domains with confidence.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

What you will take away

By the end of the series, you will have:

  • A working mental model of how Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite, the Business AI Platform, Knowledge Graph, and the SAP Family of Models fit together architecturally
  • A hands-on reference for building, governing, and observing SAP agents across the enterprise
  • A clearer view of the data and governance foundations that determine whether AI scales or stalls
  • Customer-grounded perspectives from organisations including Bayer and Henkel on what is actually working in production
  • A holistic view of how the Enterprise Architect's role evolves when AI agents become first-class citizens within the enterprise landscape

We strongly recommend attending live where possible. The Q&A in each session is where the most useful exchanges tend to happen, and the cohort across the series will include architects from across the SAP ecosystem customers, partners, and SAP experts.

Register Now: https://url.sap/ea-aewebinar

Architects will define what the Autonomous Enterprise actually looks like in production. This series is built to make sure that definition is a deliberate one.

We look forward to seeing you in the sessions.

A Big Thank you to all the collaborators and my co-leads for this series!

 @JaSoN_Luo , @ralph_richter , @PaulKurchina @VenkataRaghu_Banda @helmut_grimm @johanneseuler

entry.title: Shape What’s Next: SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 in 2026 Is Now Open
entry.content:
We’re excited to announce that Cycle 2 of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative for 2026 is now live!
This cycle features 38 new projects, offering SAP customers and partners the opportunity to gain early insights into upcoming product developments and collaborate directly with SAP teams to shape the next generation of SAP solutions.

🗓Registration is open until June 19, 2026.

In a rapidly evolving business environment, early involvement can make a meaningful difference. Through the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative, you can work directly with SAP product managers and developers, sharing your feedback on concepts, prototypes, and plans while they are still in the early stages.

To help you explore all available opportunities, we’ve prepared a complete overview of the projects.
View the full list here, or browse the links below to discover the topics most relevant to you.

SAP Business Technology Platform

  • AI-Assisted Error Remediation for SAP Application Interface Framework
  • AI assisted guidance in the UI theme designer
  • BTP Specific Data Privacy & AI based Data Masking
  • Evolution of SAP BTP Runtimes
  • Fulfillment of business needs through Business Adaption Plans and Solution Implementation
  • Improving the Experience on SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtime
  • Partner Perspectives in SAP BTP and AI development

Cloud ERP

  • Accelerate Integrations with AI powered SAP Integration Suite
  • Agentic AI in SAP S/4HANA Procurement (Private Cloud Edition)
  • Agentic AI Smart Billing Assistant
  • AI in SAP GRC for SAP HANA
  • AI-Powered Warranty Verification – Co-Innovate the future of service, repair & claims
  • Finance Intelligence on SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Intelligent Returns: Process Discovery & Automation Roadmap
  • Modernization Approaches in Purchase Order (SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition)
  • Parallel Unit of Measure

SAP Concur

  • Concur Invoice - AI-first, Reimagine Invoice Experience
  • Seamless configuration promotion for SAP Concur

SAP Globalization

  • AI for UI: Exploring the Usage of AI in Translating SAP's Product User Interface
  • Assessing Demand for AI Speech Services in SAP Translation Hub
  • PayTo Decoded: Let's Build It Together

Customer Success & Delivery

  • Improve SAP Cloud ALM Configuration & Security Analysis for SAP BTP and Cloud

Data & Analytics

  • SAP HANA Cloud Global Landscape Management
  • SAP HANA Cloud Intelligent Advisors
  • SAP HANA Database Migration to SAP HANA Cloud (XSC,XSA,CAP)

Lifecycle Management

  • Comprehensive Planning Assistant for Maintenance Planner
  • New version of SAP Add-On Assembly Kit

SAP Customer Engagement Initiative meets SAP Integration Experience Feedback

  • Hands-on: AI-powered Dispute Mgmt Scenario with SAP Enterprise Service Mgmt - Provisioning & Setup
  • Hands-on Experience of AI-powered Dispute Mgmt Scenario with SAP Enterprise Service Management
  • Hands-on System Experience of the AI-Assisted Process Modeler in SAP Signavio

SAP Learning

  • Reimagining SAP Certification: Evolving a Skills-Based and Future-Ready Certification Experience

Others

  • Autonomous Supply Chain: Embodied AI in the Warehouse
  • Embodied AI Autonomous Asset Inspection
  • Embodied AI: Autonomous Visual Inspection for Quality and Safety
  • Future of SAP Product UX
  • Providing feedback on the Discovery domain in SAP LeanIX
  • SAP Traceability Hub Repository
  • Support in Flow – Shaping Built-In Support Experience

Let’s shape the future together!

Innovation happens through collaboration. By participating in the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative projects, you help SAP create better, more relevant solutions for businesses like yours. This is your chance to influence what comes next.

Don’t miss out – sign up today at influence.sap.com and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 in 2026!

Warm regards, 
Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team 

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

entry.title: ABAP FS: Three Months Later
entry.content:
“The greatest trick ABAP FS ever pulled was convincing developers that Copilot was doing all the magic.”

A bit of background

Marcello Urbani’s ABAP Remote Filesystem extension has enabled ABAP development in VS Code since 2018. I still have no idea how he did that. He brought ABAP development to VS Code long before SAP seriously considered it.

A year ago, I started building what is now ABAP FS v2. It was supposed to be a casual side project/experiment that ended up consuming most of my time and thoughts. AI enables you to build things quickly - which is good but also bad because there’s always something more to build and it never ends!

V2 goes open-source

Three months ago, I open-sourced v2 so it could improve and benefit the entire ABAP development community (and to put my name behind something meaningful, of course).

We’re proud to share that ABAP FS now has 290+ GitHub stars and 53K activeinstalls - gaining 10K new active installs since v2 went live! And this doesn’t even include offline versions probably floating around inside enterprise environments!

What has happened since

The last three months have been very exciting.

ABAP FS has gained 15+ new features across 26 releases, including REPL, Notebooks, Heartbeat, Replay debugger, Skills, subagents and MCP support.

The community has reported 40+ issues, of which 15 were real bugs that have since been fixed.

We’ve also had 4 new contributors - Gurjant Singh, Özgür, Oláh Tamás, and Sébastien along with 4 new positive reviews in the VS Code extension marketplace.

The rough edges

AI enables you to move faster and experiment easily - not every feature is perfect and that’s part of the process. Features like the Notebooks and replay debugger are as much about proving what’s possible for ABAP as they’re about solving problems today. Once you know something is possible, even if it’s not perfect, it opens the door for the community and maybe even SAP to build something better!

What’s next

ABAP FS currently only supports Basic authentication which is a problem. So we’re adding support for other authentication methods like SSO, Kerberos, etc - coming soon!

I also feel the need to address both the elephants in the room:

SAP’s new API policy - Everyone’s talking about it and there are several interpretations of it depending on who you ask. If you ask me, the policy restricts accessing real business data using ADT APIs but you can use them for their intended purpose, which is ABAP development. Until SAP makes the position clearer, I think it’s safer to proceed carefully and stick to development scenarios.

SAP’s official VS Code extension - Finally. From what I hear, it’s gonna be free to use and will eventually/hopefully have full parity with Eclipse ADT. I hope it’s good and useful. Yes, I will probably be sad if and when ABAP FS becomes unnecessary but I hope it has a meaningful purpose while it’s alive - make ABAP development faster, more intelligent, and less frustrating.

Thank you

First - Thank you Marcello. None of this would be possible without you. Thank you for sharing your work and your expertise with the world! Collaborating with you has been a pleasure and a privilege!

A huge thanks to Gurjant Singh, Özgür, Oláh Tamás, and Sébastien for your contributions.

Thanks to everyone who’ve tried ABAP FS, reviewed it, reported issues, provided feedback, blogged, and told friends and colleagues about it.

We would love to hear how you’ve been using ABAP FS, what’s working well and especially what’s not!

Homepage: https://marcellourbani.github.io/vscode_abap_remote_fs/

Marketplace: https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=murbani.vscode-abap-remote-fs

Repo: https://github.com/marcellourbani/vscode_abap_remote_fs
