entry.title: A Look Back on my SAP Sapphire 2026 Experience
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire 2026 Orlando is officially in the books, and I am still riding the high from one of the most energizing weeks of the year. Between presenting the SAP HANA Cloud overview session and working the HANA Cloud booth, I had the pleasure to connect with hundreds of data professionals — answering questions, sparking conversations, and sharing potential database solutions. Here is a quick recap of what went down for me.

The Session: AI Starts with the Right Database

This overview presentation tackled two critical challenges that data teams are facing right now. First, the importance of bringing together every type and size of dataset in an organization — structured, unstructured, relational, graph, vector, spatial — in a way that actually makes AI smarter. Second, equipping data professionals with AI-driven tools so they can ship solutions faster without sacrificing quality.

As a single, cloud-native, in-memory database engine, SAP HANA Cloud supports analytics, applications, and AI from one platform. Its multi-model architecture means a singular database instance can simultaneously serve a transactional sales process, offer product recommendations using a vector engine, guide the customer experience using a knowledge graph, and increase operational efficiency thanks to spatial awareness. Additionally, the newly released SAP HANA Cloud agents reduce the SQL complexity and due diligence time that typically slows development teams down, enabling natural language access to database objects and relationships through an autogenerated knowledge graph.

The SAP HANA Cloud Booth: Four Questions That Kept Coming Back

Working the HANA Cloud booth proved just as rewarding as the session. Four questions surfaced repeatedly, and they are worth sharing here.

  • What is SAP Business Data Cloud? SAP Business Data Cloud is a suite of data management solutions — including SAP Datasphere, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Business Warehouse, and SAP Databricks — that work together to enable a business data fabric. Customers purchase BDC capacity units and apply them across these solutions based on their needs.
  • What does it mean that SAP HANA Cloud is now in SAP Business Data Cloud? HANA Cloud is an in-memory, multi-tiered, multi-model database capable of powering transactional applications, analytics, data science workflows, and AI use cases. Its inclusion in Business Data Cloud means customers now have access to a complete, unified set of data solutions.
  • How is SAP S/4HANA different from SAP HANA Cloud? S/4HANA is an ERP cloud solution built on a version of the HANA database. HANA Cloud is a standalone database that customers provision to power their own custom applications, analytics, or AI solutions.
  • Do I need SAP Business Data Cloud for AI? Absolutely. The effectiveness of any AI solution is directly tied to the quality, quantity, and quickness of the data behind it. SAP Business Data Cloud weaves together all data — SAP and non-SAP — into a singular business data fabric that is ready for Joule and other AI solutions. From a bigger picture perspective, the three domains of applications, data, and AI are intertwining so tightly that any one cannot exist effectively without the other two.

Keep the Momentum Going

If SAP Sapphire sparked your curiosity, do not let it stop there. Explore the free SAP HANA Cloud basic trial or the free SAP Business Data Cloud trial to get hands-on experience with these data solutions.

Have a topic about SAP HANA Cloud that is still fuzzy? Join the Data Professionals Community to learn, share, and grow with other data enthusiasts. Then, drop that question in the comments or create a discussion post — the conversation is just getting started.

entry.title: SAP AI Adoption and Learning Week – From Vision to Enterprise Application
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming businesses, but how do you move from theory to real-world application? Join SAP AI Adoption and Learning Week (July 6–9) to explore AI’s practical impact on enterprise solutions.

This four-day virtual event brings together business leaders, developers, and consultants for expert-led sessions, live demos, and hands-on exercises. Discover how to:

  • Learn AI fundamentals in SAP solutions
  • Practice with real business scenarios
  • Apply AI in your enterprise architecture
  • Adopt AI at scale with confidence

Why Attend?

  • Gain insights from AI experts and real-world use cases
  • Participate in interactive CodeJams and system demos
  • Understand how to integrate AI into existing workflows
  • Get actionable strategies for AI adoption

Save the Date: July 6–9 Pre-register now to stay updated when registration opens. Don’t miss this opportunity to turn AI vision into business reality!

Pre-register here

Key Takeaways 

  • Understand AI’s enterprise impact – Learn how AI transforms business processes in SAP solutions.
  • Apply AI in real scenarios – Work through end-to-end business cases and live system demos.
  • Integrate AI into existing workflows – Discover how AI fits into enterprise architecture.
  • Scale AI adoption – Get strategies to activate and deploy AI in production environments.
  • Build trust in AI – Learn best practices for operationalizing AI responsibly.

entry.title: Joule Work | Insights
entry.content:
Joule Work brings together AI assistants that help guide decisions and agents that can take action on behalf of users in a single workspace, designed to simplify how people engage with SAP day to day.

Instead of switching between applications and navigating across systems, users can express what they’re trying to accomplish and let the system coordinate the next steps—surfacing relevant information, triggering workflows, and handling routine tasks behind the scenes.

It addresses a real source of friction. Much of today’s enterprise work still depends on knowing where things live, what system to open, and what process to follow. Moving to a more intent-driven interaction model reduces that burden.

The result is a more direct path from “what needs to happen” to “what actually gets done.” As mentioned in #Sapphire2026 " One place to direct the entire business.

Value proposition

  • Transform work with a proactive, outcome-driven user experience. People express a goal and AI orchestrates actions across systems to get it done.
  • Help people spend less time switching applications​ and connecting workflows with a cohesive experience ​that spans your enterprise systems.
  • Speed up processes with proactive assistance that uses ​the deep business context of Joule Agents and Joule Assistants ​to surface relevant data and recommend actions.
  • Keep teams focused on driving decisions and outcomes​ with adaptive, goal-driven workflows that delegate routine ​work to supervised agents.

Check out this link for more information and you can also request a demo Joule Work 

#SAPBusiness AI #JouleWork #SAP Artificial Intelligence Mobile 

Short video

Regards

Shubham

entry.title: Intelligent Error Handling in SAP CPI Using AI Adapter
entry.content:
Introduction:

SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) provides powerful integration capabilities, but analyzing runtime exceptions and identifying resolutions often requires manual effort. With the new AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite, we can now build intelligent error handling flows that automatically analyze exceptions using Large Language Models (LLMs) and return meaningful resolutions back to the source system.

In this blog, we will build an AI-powered error handling mechanism where:

  • CPI captures runtime exceptions
  • Sends the error text to an AI model using the AI Adapter
  • AI analyzes the issue
  • Returns Cause and Resolution
  • CPI sends the intelligent response back to the sender

 

Traditionally, when an iFlow fails, support teams manually analyze:

    • MPL logs
    • Exception stack traces
    • Adapter errors
    • HTTP failures

This process can be time-consuming for support teams and integration developers.

Using AI, we can automate:

      • Error classification
      • Root cause analysis
      • Suggested resolution
      • Troubleshooting guidance

This helps reduce support effort and improves response time.

Architecture Overview

Flow:

Sender → Exception Subprocess → AI Adapter → AI Model → Response Back to Sender

The Exception Subprocess captures the CPI runtime error and forwards the error text to an LLM through the AI Adapter.

The AI model then generates:

        • Error Summary
        • Root Cause
        • Resolution Steps

 

Prerequisites

Before starting, ensure the following:

          • SAP Integration Suite tenant
          • AI Adapter available in CPI
          • API Key for OpenAI/OpenRouter
          • AI Receiver Adapter configured

In this example, we use OpenRouter free models for testing purposes.

Step 1: Create Security Material

Navigate to:

Monitor → Security Material

Create:

  • Type: Secure Parameter
  • Name: OPENROUTER_API_KEY

Value:

  • Bearer sk-or-v1-xxxxxxxx

 

Step 2: Configure AI Adapter

Add an AI Receiver Adapter inside the Exception Subprocess.

Adapter Configuration

 

Address

OpenRouter URL/OpenAI URL

Resource Path

/api/v1/chat/completions

HTTP Method

POST

Authentication

API Key Alias- Created in Step 1

 

 

 

 

 

Step 3: Configure Request Body

Use the following JSON payload:

{
  "model": "openai/gpt-4",
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "system",
      "content": "You are an SAP CPI support expert."
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "Analyze this SAP CPI error and provide Error, Cause, and Resolution: ${property.CamelExceptionCaught}"
    }
  ],
  "temperature": 0.3,
  "max_tokens": 400
}

 

 

Understanding the Roles

system Role

Defines AI behavior.

Example:

{
  "role": "system",
  "content": "You are an SAP CPI support expert."
}

This helps the model provide SAP-specific troubleshooting responses.

user Role

Contains the actual exception text from CPI.

Example:

{
  "role": "user",
  "content": "Analyze this SAP CPI error..."
}

 

Step 4: Capture Exception in Exception Subprocess

Inside the Exception Subprocess:

  • Add Content Modifier
  • Store exception into property:

                    ${exception.message} or ${property.CamelExceptionCaught}

  This property is then passed to the AI Adapter.

Step 5: Return AI Response to Sender

After AI Adapter execution:

  • Use Content Modifier or Groovy Script
  • Extract AI response
  • Return formatted response to sender

Example output:

Error:
Invalid endpoint configuration

Cause:
The HTTP receiver adapter contains an incorrect URL path.

Resolution:
Verify the endpoint path and ensure the API resource URL is valid.

 

 

 

 

Sample Use Cases

This approach can be used for:

    • HTTP adapter failures
    • SOAP faults
    • OData authentication errors
    • Mapping exceptions
    • XML parsing issues
    • API throttling analysis
    • Runtime deployment failures

 

Benefits of AI-Based Error Handling

Faster Troubleshooting

Developers receive instant suggestions without manually checking logs.

Reduced Support Effort

Support teams can automate first-level analysis.

Improved Developer Productivity

AI provides contextual recommendations and possible fixes.

Better Monitoring Experience

Instead of cryptic stack traces, users receive readable explanations.

entry.title: Is SAP Still a Software Company? Reflections from Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
Like many of you, we attended SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026 last week. Before it, our minds were already set on the most likely topics the conversations would revolve around. We thought about the usual when it comes to enterprise AI themes: copilots, new generative capabilities, maybe the launching of something unexpected. Of course, those topics were covered, and we were right to assume that they would be the center of the event. However, the keynote didn’t waste any time and soon revealed that we were there for something much bigger; we were there to witness the first steps towards a bigger strategic shift that is taking place inside SAP. The discussion was no longer just about adding AI features into enterprise software, but about redefining the role ERP systems could play in the next generation of enterprise AI execution.

Over the past two years, most organizations have been implementing copilots that help employees be more productive. For instance, employees can send emails faster, write meeting minutes faster, and look for information easier. However, once enterprises attempt to implement AI on a larger scale, it becomes clear how difficult it is for businesses to actually make things work. Recommendations can be made but putting them into practice in an environment full of disparate systems, workflows, approvals, and data sources can be extremely hard.

This is precisely why SAP's approach to AI seems so unique. Instead of presenting AI as another layer separate from the core of enterprise operations, SAP is attempting to integrate AI into the very systems where business processes take place. As one of the speakers emphasized during their keynote presentation, ERP is "the brain of every company." This idea formed the basis for all the presentations at Sapphire. SAP's view on enterprise AI is that it becomes much more valuable when it operates with process awareness, governance, semantic business context, and operational data.

One of the key announcements made at Sapphire is SAP Business AI Platform. Surprisingly, SAP did not introduce it as "another AI offering". This product is positioned as an architectural layer connecting enterprise AI to operational systems and business processes. The purpose here is to merge ERP process knowledge, Business Data Cloud, AI orchestration, governance frameworks, and semantic business context into a single enterprise AI layer for executing actions, not just giving suggestions.

Another dominant topic at the Sapphire event was moving from AI assistants to operational AI agents. SAP introduced hundreds of AI agents and assistants for Finance, Procurement, Supply Chain, HCM, and Customer Experience processes. However, the number of them was not really the point of their announcement. Much greater attention was paid to orchestration. SAP clearly communicated that enterprise AI will not succeed with isolated copilots, but rather with integrated AI systems that can interact in operational processes. In other words, the real challenge today is not just implementing AI solutions. The goal is to achieve operationalizing them within the enterprise.

Especially fascinating about this approach is its implications for enterprise architecture going forward. Traditionally, enterprises have always treated enterprise software as an assembly of individual platforms that can be integrated. But now, AI is starting to reveal the limitations of such an approach. The more fragmented the operational environment becomes, the less effective it is for AI algorithms to work. This is likely the reason behind SAP’s long focus on topics such as Business Data Cloud, semantic layer, governance models, and process orchestration. It looks like SAP believes that the future success of enterprise AI lies not in how well the individual models perform but rather in the level of their connectivity and operational awareness.

This also signals an important shift for SAP partners and customers. Until recently, discussions around digital transformation revolved around implementation, migration, and modernization. But as enterprise AI becomes more prominent, the focus is shifting to operational readiness. Clean data structures, process standardization, governance models, and ERP systems become prerequisites for successful AI adoption rather than just IT requirements. In a way, Sapphire 2026 suggests that the most capable adopters of AI are not necessarily those experimenting with the greatest number of copilots, but those with the most robust operational foundations to support them.

Following Sapphire 2026, it becomes increasingly clear that SAP tries to evolve beyond being defined as a software company. It seeks to position itself as the operational layer for AI, merging ERP logic, enterprise data, governance, and AI orchestration into a single architecture. If this vision is going to materialize is yet to be seen, but the direction is hard to deny. SAP believes that the next wave of competitive advantages in enterprise AI does not lie in the strength of the AI models but in the ownership of the operational systems that execute AI-driven business decisions.

entry.title: The Patient SAP Technical Architect
entry.content:
I have spent years writing things no human will ever read. Behavior definitions. CDS annotations. Authorization objects with names like F_BKPF_BUK. My work lived in layers below the surface of the enterprise — invisible, load-bearing, essential. The user clicked a button. I was the reason it worked. We never met.

SAP Joule is the first thing in thirty years of SAP's history that changes that relationship. Not for the user — they always had the button. It changes it for me, the architect. Joule is, in some sense, a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote.

I. What It Actually Is

Let us be precise before we are poetic.

Joule is SAP's generative AI copilot, available since late 2023 across S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Analytics Cloud, and BTP. It sits as a panel on the right side of your Fiori Launchpad. You type a question in plain language. It answers using your live SAP data — not a simulation, not a demonstration dataset, not a hallucination dressed in corporate clothing.

The architecture underneath is worth understanding. There are large language models — partnerships with Mistral, Anthropic, and others — running inside SAP AI Core on BTP. But the models alone are not what makes Joule interesting. What makes it interesting is the grounding layer: a proprietary business context engine that tethers every LLM response to real SAP master data, transactional records, and configuration. The authorization layer then filters that response through your role's existing permission objects.

Joule will not invent a vendor name. It will not fabricate an invoice amount. It reads what is actually in your system, through the authorizations you actually hold. This is the thing that separates it from every consumer AI product your CFO has already tried to ban.

Retrieval Augmented Generation fetches SAP Help documentation and live business data at query time. The result is a response grounded in both SAP's knowledge and your company's reality simultaneously. For an architect who has spent years building that reality — one CDS view at a time — this is not a small thing.

II. What Changes for the Developer

In Business Application Studio, Joule is embedded as an intelligent coding assistant that understands not just syntax, but intent within SAP's specific frameworks. This is the distinction that matters.

A general coding assistant knows JavaScript. Joule knows CAP. It knows that a @readonly annotation in a projection entity has specific implications for the generated OData service. It knows RAP — managed and unmanaged scenarios, the difference between a BDEF with managed implementation and one with a handler class. It knows Fiori annotations well enough to suggest @ui.lineItem positioning without being told the rendering context.

Consider what used to take an afternoon:

-- A developer once spent 3-4 hours writing:
-- Entity definition, associations, access controls,
-- projection view, service definition, BDEF,
-- behavior implementation stubs.

-- Now the prompt is:
"Generate a managed RAP Business Object for Equipment
Maintenance. Header has Equipment, Description, PlantCode,
Category. Items have Task, PlannedDate, Status. Include
mandatory field validations and a confirmMaintenance action."

-- Joule produces all of it. In seconds.
Time savings documented in SAP's own benchmarks:
CDS view generation 2–4 hours → 15 minutes.
RAP BDEF generation 3–5 hours → 30 minutes.
Unit test generation 2–3 hours → 20 minutes.

The productivity numbers are real. But productivity is the least interesting thing happening here.

What is actually happening is a shift in what the developer's day consists of. If Joule generates the scaffolding, I am no longer spending my cognitive energy on the ceremony of SAP development — the syntax rituals, the framework boilerplate, the documentation archaeology. I am spending it on the decisions that actually require judgment: data modeling choices, authorization concept design, integration topology, performance trade-offs that the LLM cannot make without business context only I possess.

The parts of the job that were tedious are becoming automated. The parts that required taste are becoming more visible.

III. What Changes for the Architect

The technical architect's role has always been translation. Business says: we need to track equipment maintenance across three plants with approval workflows. The architect translates that into a set of data structures, authorization concepts, integration points, and deployment decisions. Then the architect communicates that translation downward to developers and upward to stakeholders.

Joule compresses the translation loop.

An architect can now ask Joule to explain the current purchasing organization structure of a client system in plain language — not to learn it themselves, but to generate documentation that previously would have taken a day to write. An architect can ask Joule to review a CDS view for performance issues and receive back a structured analysis with specific optimization suggestions. An architect can use Joule in Joule Studio to build custom AI skills that extend Joule's capabilities to the client's specific domain — fleet management, specialized procurement categories, industry-specific compliance — without writing LLM infrastructure from scratch.

The architect who understands Joule's grounding architecture — RAG over SAP content, live data APIs, role-based authorization filtering — can design systems that work with the AI layer rather than around it. This is the new literacy.

Joule Studio is worth dwelling on. It is SAP's low-code environment for building custom AI skills — defining intents, entities, actions, and knowledge bases that extend Joule to domains SAP itself has not anticipated. An architect who can define a skill scope, enumerate the 15 most common user intents, connect them to CAP service endpoints, and upload a policy knowledge base for RAG-based answering is delivering something qualitatively different from the architects who cannot.

The custom skill architecture is clean in its separation of concerns: user request → intent recognition → skill routing → backend API call → structured result → natural language response. Every piece of this maps onto patterns architects already know. The new piece is the intent layer. Learning to design for intent rather than for transaction is the shift.

IV. On Security and the Things Clients Ask

Every architect deploying Joule will face the same questions in the same order. They deserve direct answers.

Does our data go to OpenAI? No. Joule runs LLMs inside SAP AI Core — SAP's tenanted infrastructure on BTP. Your data does not reach public AI providers. The BTP data center region you select determines data residency.

Can users see what they shouldn't? No. Joule inherits your existing SAP authorization objects. The same XSUAA integration and IAM role collections that govern Fiori app access govern what Joule can retrieve and display. An AP clerk asking Joule about payroll data receives the same result as that clerk opening the payroll app directly: nothing.

Does Joule learn from our data and share it? No. Tenant isolation is strict. Your interaction data does not train shared models.

Can Joule take destructive actions? Only within the user's authorization scope, and with explicit confirmation for significant actions. Joule does not bypass workflow approvals. It can initiate them.

The governance framework an architect should recommend to clients has five components: an AI usage policy defining acceptable prompts, a data classification guide for what belongs in prompt text, audit logging via SAP Audit Log Service, access control via BTP Role Collections, and a custom skill review process before any new skills reach production.

This is not exotic. It is the same governance discipline good architects have always applied to system access. The vocabulary is new. The principles are familiar.

V. The Prompting Problem

There is a skill that SAP calls the CRAFT framework — Context, Role, Action, Format, Target — for constructing effective prompts. Architects deploying Joule will find themselves teaching this to end users. They will also need to build what the guide calls a Prompt Library: a collection of validated, role-specific prompts that become a deliverable of the implementation project itself.

This is not trivial. A prompt is a specification. A bad specification produces bad output. The work of prompt engineering is, at its core, the same work as requirements engineering: making implicit intent explicit enough that a system can act on it reliably.

-- Vague (produces vague output):
"Show me sales data"

-- Precise (produces precise output):
"As the Regional Sales Manager, summarize in a table
the top 10 customers by overdue receivables for
company code 1000 as of today, showing amount,
days overdue, and responsible account manager."

Architects who understand why good prompts work — because they reduce the search space of possible interpretations — will build better prompt libraries. They will also build better training programs, because they can explain the underlying principle rather than just distributing a list of magic phrases.

VI. What the Roadmap Actually Means

SAP has stated its intention to make Joule the primary interface for all SAP applications by 2026. The roadmap includes agentic AI — Joule executing multi-step business processes autonomously — and cross-application orchestration: one prompt triggering actions in S/4HANA, Ariba, and SAC simultaneously without human mediation at each step.

Architects should read this carefully.

Agentic AI changes the error model. When a human executes a sequence of SAP transactions, each step is a checkpoint. When an agent executes them, the checkpoints are only where you designed them. The architect who builds agentic workflows must think carefully about where confirmation steps live, what constitutes a reversible versus irreversible action, and how audit trails are maintained across a sequence initiated by a single natural language instruction.

This is not a reason to be afraid of agentic AI. It is a reason for architects to be involved in its design rather than observing from outside. The governance questions — what can an agent do without human confirmation, what triggers a human-in-the-loop, how do we reconcile agent actions with existing workflow approval hierarchies — are architectural questions. They require people who understand both the business process and the technical substrate.

The architects who will matter most in the next five years are not the ones who resist AI augmentation. They are the ones who understand it precisely enough to design its guardrails.

VII. A Note on What Disappears

When a senior user of SAP needs to find the payment status of a vendor invoice, they navigate to FBL1N, enter the vendor number, set the selection criteria, execute the report, read the results. This takes two minutes if you know what you are doing. It takes fifteen minutes if you do not, and it takes a phone call to IT if you have forgotten the T-code entirely.

Joule collapses this to: "What is the payment status for vendor Bosch?"

The T-code does not disappear. The CDS view behind the Fiori app that FBL1N has become does not disappear. The authorization object F_LFA1_BUK that controls whether this user can see this vendor's data does not disappear. Everything I built is still there. What disappears is the requirement that the user know any of it.

This is the correct outcome. Users should never have needed to know T-codes. That was always an implementation detail leaking into the user experience — a failure of interface design that we normalized because we had no better option. Joule is the better option, thirty years late.

For the developer, this means the contract changes. I am no longer primarily responsible for navigation design or information architecture in the traditional sense. I am responsible for the quality of the data models and service definitions that Joule reads. If my CDS associations are poorly structured, Joule's answers will be poorly structured. If my authorization concepts are coarse-grained, Joule's data filtering will be coarse-grained. The user never sees my code. They see Joule's response to my code. The distance between my work and its consequence has compressed.

I began by saying that Joule is a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote. That is not quite right. More precisely: Joule is the interface through which the things I built finally learned to speak for themselves.

The patient labor of enterprise software architecture — the years of CDS views and RAP objects and authorization concepts — was always in service of business processes that required this complexity to function. The complexity was never the goal. The goal was always the vendor getting paid, the employee going on leave, the purchase order reaching the right supplier on time.

Joule does not make the complexity disappear. It makes the complexity invisible, which is what the complexity always wanted to be. My job, as architect, is to ensure the invisible infrastructure is worthy of the interface that now speaks for it.

That is not a smaller job. It is, finally, the right one.

entry.title: Get Early Insights to SAP AI Innovations: Join SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 by June 19
entry.content:
At SAP Sapphire, SAP reaffirmed its strong commitment to AI-driven innovation and its role in transforming businesses.

Innovation at SAP is built on collaboration. Leveraging cutting-edge technologies like Artificial Intelligence, SAP continues to drive meaningful business value for customers. Now, we invite you to be part of this journey by participating in SAP Customer Engagement Initiative projects focused on AI.

🗓Registration is open until June 19, 2026.

In today’s fast-evolving business landscape, early involvement is key. Through the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative, you can collaborate directly with SAP product managers and developers, sharing feedback on concepts, prototypes, and innovations while they are still taking shape.

View the full list here, or browse the links below to discover the selected exciting opportunities:

Let’s shape the future together!

Innovation happens through collaboration. By participating in the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative projects, you help SAP create better, more relevant solutions for businesses like yours. This is your chance to influence what comes next.

Don’t miss out – sign up today at influence.sap.com and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 in 2026!

Warm regards, 
Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team 

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

entry.title: Unlock Better Hiring with Qualifications: Start Smart, Scale Faster with SAP Fieldglass
entry.content:
Why Qualifications Matter

Qualifications bring structure and consistency to hiring by replacing free-text job requirements with clearly defined skills. When used consistently, they improve candidate matching, enable better system recommendations, and create a reusable framework that scales with your organization. Without this structure, job postings vary widely and AI features lack the context needed to be effective.

The Hidden Cost of Skipping Day 1 Setup

Features like Recommend Qualifications depend entirely on an existing qualification library. Without it, the system has no reference point, and recommendations cannot work. Creating this library manually can feel time-consuming, which is why many teams delay it—and often never return to it.

The Solution: Generate Qualifications from Existing Data

Instead of starting from scratch, you can use Generate Qualifications to build your library instantly using AI. The system analyzes your existing job postings and templates, extracts commonly used skills, and creates a recommended list of qualifications. In one step, you move from having no structure to a usable, standardized framework.

What You Need to Do (Day One Setup)

  1. Upload your job postings or templates
  2. Contact your Configuration Manager to run Generate Qualifications
  3. Review and select the relevant qualifications

This creates a ready-to-use qualification library that supports both job postings and recommendations.

Activating Recommend Qualifications

Once your library is in place, enable the Recommend Qualifications feature. During job posting creation, the system suggests relevant qualifications based on your job description—using your own structured data.

Key insight: The quality of recommendations depends on the quality of your library.

How This Improves Hiring

With qualifications in place, recruiters can create consistent job postings, rely on better recommendations, and improve candidate matching. This leads to faster, more accurate hiring decisions without adding complexity.

Final Thoughts

Qualifications are a foundational step in skill-based hiring. By setting them up early using Generate Qualifications, you can quickly unlock the value of AI features and create a more consistent, scalable hiring process.

Start with the data you already have, build your qualification library, and let the system do the rest.

You can also have a look at the following topic in the What’s New Viewer for SAP Fieldglass for more details: 

AI Assisted Qualification Generation for Qualification Library
